introSection: Olympia Looping is a steel travelling roller coaster with five inversions owned by R. Barth and Sohn KG, the family business of the late manufacturer Rudolf Barth.

sections.title: About
sections.content:
Olympia Looping is the largest portable roller coaster in the world, with a track length of and a mass of 900 tonnes. The ride features five vertical loops that are positioned to look like the Olympic rings. The loops are more circular than on most roller coasters; this creates higher g-forces of up to 5.2 g. It was designed by Schwarzkopf and Ing.-Büro Stengel GmbH and fabricated by BHS. The pneumatic and control systems were provided by Gerstlauer Elektro GmbH.

The ride is commonly found at large carnivals and fairs in Germany, most notably the Munich Oktoberfest. In recent times, it has seen more semi-permanent locations, such as Prater in Austria. Since 2016, the ride has traveled to the UK for the Hyde Park Winter Wonderland in London, where it operates under the name of Munich Looping.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride debuted at the 1989 edition of Oktoberfest.

In 2013, Olympia Looping underwent a renovation that included a full repaint of the track and trains, alongside an upgrade to its lighting system, replacing the original lights with LEDs.

In March 2016, it was announced that the coaster would be housed at Prater for its summer season to celebrate the park's 250th Anniversary, making it the first time the coaster would have a semi-permanent home, up until that year's Oktoberfest. The coaster has made many appearances at Prater up until August 2025, when the park announced that it would likely be the last time that it would operate at Prater due to a theatre being built on the site where it normally sits. It ran that year from 15 March-24 August.

Olympia Looping was semi-confirmed as appearing at Hyde Park Winter Wonderland on 5 November 2016 after pieces of the roller coaster's track were seen being driven through London. This was later followed by the coaster beginning to be constructed at Hyde Park. The roller coaster operated under the name Munich Looping while in London. It was the first time the roller coaster left mainland Europe. It has been present at every Winter Wonderland since.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On 27 September 2008, a driving motor failed on the ride, stranding over 20 Oktoberfest attendees at the top of the first hill. They were freed with the help of the Munich fire department.

sections.title: Theme
sections.content: Olympia Looping is not the only Olympics-themed roller coaster -the travelling roller coaster Jet Star has a Winter Olympic theme.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: Usually, the Olympia Looping uses 5-car trains when not operating at Munich Oktoberfest. However at Hyde Park Winter Wonderland in London, UK the additional cars were brought over.

fullText:
Olympia Looping is a steel travelling roller coaster with five inversions owned by R. Barth and Sohn KG, the family business of the late manufacturer Rudolf Barth.

Olympia Looping is the largest portable roller coaster in the world, with a track length of and a mass of 900 tonnes. The ride features five vertical loops that are positioned to look like the Olympic rings. The loops are more circular than on most roller coasters; this creates higher g-forces of up to 5.2 g. It was designed by Schwarzkopf and Ing.-Büro Stengel GmbH and fabricated by BHS. The pneumatic and control systems were provided by Gerstlauer Elektro GmbH.

The ride is commonly found at large carnivals and fairs in Germany, most notably the Munich Oktoberfest. In recent times, it has seen more semi-permanent locations, such as Prater in Austria. Since 2016, the ride has traveled to the UK for the Hyde Park Winter Wonderland in London, where it operates under the name of Munich Looping.

The ride debuted at the 1989 edition of Oktoberfest.

In 2013, Olympia Looping underwent a renovation that included a full repaint of the track and trains, alongside an upgrade to its lighting system, replacing the original lights with LEDs.

In March 2016, it was announced that the coaster would be housed at Prater for its summer season to celebrate the park's 250th Anniversary, making it the first time the coaster would have a semi-permanent home, up until that year's Oktoberfest. The coaster has made many appearances at Prater up until August 2025, when the park announced that it would likely be the last time that it would operate at Prater due to a theatre being built on the site where it normally sits. It ran that year from 15 March-24 August.

Olympia Looping was semi-confirmed as appearing at Hyde Park Winter Wonderland on 5 November 2016 after pieces of the roller coaster's track were seen being driven through London. This was later followed by the coaster beginning to be constructed at Hyde Park. The roller coaster operated under the name Munich Looping while in London. It was the first time the roller coaster left mainland Europe. It has been present at every Winter Wonderland since.

On 27 September 2008, a driving motor failed on the ride, stranding over 20 Oktoberfest attendees at the top of the first hill. They were freed with the help of the Munich fire department.



Olympia Looping is not the only Olympics-themed roller coaster -the travelling roller coaster Jet Star has a Winter Olympic theme.



Usually, the Olympia Looping uses 5-car trains when not operating at Munich Oktoberfest. However at Hyde Park Winter Wonderland in London, UK the additional cars were brought over.




 * Olympia Looping's website
